data.title: Victoria's Secret Q1 Preview: Poison Pill Narrative Should Dominate The Call
data.description:
Introduction

Victoria's Secret & Co. (NYSE:VSCO) is due to report its Q1 numbers, which I don’t think will blow anyone’s minds on the 5th of June, before the market opens, so I wanted to go through some of those numbers and talk about what may be the main talking point on the conference call, if there will still be no resolution until then.

What to Expect

Analysts expect the company to report 4 cents in adjusted EPS, as well as a loss of 2 cents in GAAP EPS, on $1.32B in sales. This is very typical of the company, as its strongest quarters are at the end of the year, which includes the holiday season, sending the company’s sales through the roof, only to come down to around these levels for the next three quarters, and the cycle repeats. How do these numbers stack up against what the management guided for the upcoming quarter? They are more or less in line with analysts. The management expects the company to make between $1.3B to $1.33B, while adjusted EPS forecast ranges from -10 cents to +10 cents. A wide margin.

How likely is the company to beat the consensus estimates? In the last ten quarters, the company beat sales estimates 50% of the time, with the last 4 being beat, as well as beating or at least meeting on EPS 70% of the time, as per below:

It seems that the analysts are underestimating the company’s potential in these down-season quarters, which could mean we will see a beat once again. Given the wide range in EPS, I could see analysts diverge from what the company is going to really report.

Overall, I am not expecting a blowout quarter, since it is not the quarter that will show good performance. The previous quarter is the money-making quarter, so in the following quarters, the company just needs to stay afloat until the next holiday season, which is a bit unfortunate. I would like to see steady, profitable performance throughout the year.

What I’m Looking Out For

The main talking about I will be looking for is, of course, the poison pill and takeover defense from the management. Just a few days ago, the management announced that an investment firm controlled by an Australian billionaire, Brett Blundy, is looking to essentially do a hostile takeover of the company. His investment firm already acquired around a 13% stake in the company and recently filed with the SEC, which may allow him to acquire 49.99% of the company.

So, what is going to happen now is that, to avoid this hostile takeover, on May 29th, the company will allow its current shareholders to purchase additional shares in the company at a significant discount if the investment firm is going to acquire at least 15% of the company. This way, only the shareholders other than the investment firm will be allowed to purchase these additional shares, in turn diluting the company further while making BBRC’s (the investment firm) stake smaller and smaller. The company is pushing back against BBRC, and adopting the poison pill method seems to be the only way of surviving the hostile takeover.

The board says that the move will protect all shareholders from losing control to a single investor, citing BBRC specifically and its history in acquiring controlling interests in other retail brands, even launching its own competing lingerie business.

So, what’s next? BBRC is now stuck. Currently sitting at a 13% stake, and if it wants to go ahead and acquire more, it is risking triggering the poison pill. The market will be watching this unfold over the next week or so. Probably even longer, which means there is a chance that the company will address this problem in its upcoming quarter earnings.

Closing Comments

The next couple of weeks will be quite interesting to see what is going to happen overall. I expect to see some sort of agreement between the board and the investment firm BBRC. The company’s share price has tumbled quite a bit since mid-December, so it makes sense that BBRC is becoming a bit more active. The company is in a period of transformation under the new CEO, Hillary Super, who took the reins not even a year ago. She is trying to breathe some life back into the company’s main brand as well as PINK. She also aims to expand more internationally and improve the overall experience, whether that is through in-store or digital.

For now, I will be watching this unfold over the next while on the sidelines, as these types of events don’t come very often.

data.title: 2 BDCs That Qualify For The Retirement Income League
data.description:
Typically, BDCs (or business development companies) are not associated with retirement income investing. This is because the level of risk that is embedded in the BDCs is simply too high for what could be considered prudent in the context of retirement income.

At its core, retirement income investing is about income durability, predictable growth, and inflation-indexation. It is certainly not about squeezing the maximum juice out of dollars invested and capturing superior returns at all costs. When investing for retirement (durable) income, risk management comes first, and then only the return component kicks in.

Now, the BDCs could be viewed as the total opposite of what I just mentioned. As the BDCs issue private credit loans to businesses that are quite aggressive in their financial moves (e.g., large-scale M&A, LBO) and cannot access cheap debt from traditional banks, it is quite clear that the underlying risks here are rather high.

However, if we for a moment forget about the risk side and think about the positives, then I see many interesting areas/aspects, where retirement or, in fact, any income-seeking portfolios could benefit:

  • Double-digit dividend yield.
  • Income that is relatively weakly correlated to the common income-producing assets (e.g., REITs, MLPs).
  • Slow and steady price appreciation potential.

Unfortunately, the risks of many BDCs outweigh the offered benefits. In other words, BDC risks are unacceptable for most retirement income investors. For example, I would imagine that a dividend cut is something that prudent investors do not want to see happening in their portfolios.

Yet, in my humble opinion, there are some positive exceptions, where the underlying level of risk is actually low enough to make the BDC suitable for even the most conservative income investors.

Let me elaborate on two such cases.

#1 - Main Street Capital

Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) is, in my view, the most defensive BDC in the sector. It has not cut the dividend since the listing, which includes the GFC, the Pandemic, the oil shock, surging interest rates, etc.

Currently, MAIN offers a yield of 7.2%, which could be considered sustainable and safe going forward (just as in the past).

I will give you the key reasons why I am so confident in MAIN.

First, MAIN invests in the highest quality private credit areas in a diversified fashion. For example, the loan book consists of about 51% LMM (lower middle market) investments, 37% MAIN directly originated investments, 4% middle market, and 8% of multiple unrelated investments. Almost all of these are placed in the first-lien category (i.e., the safest in the BDC game). Furthermore, MAIN has a separate asset management arm, which generates passive fees that are driven by the managed asset base rather than direct credit investment performance. This is a significant tailwind for having a non-cyclical portfolio.

Second, the weighted average senior leverage of MAIN's investments stands at 2.8x EBITDA, and senior interest coverage is at 2.4x EBITDA. The non-accruals have also been consistently below the 2% level (FV basis). All of these statistics are materially better than what we can see for any average BDC.

Third, and this is what makes MAIN rather unique. MAIN has a combination of one of the lowest leverage levels in the sector and also one of the strongest dividend coverage levels. Namely, the debt to equity is 0.79x, which is below the sector average of 1.19x. The base dividend coverage is 143%, which is way above the sector average of 103%.

Finally, MAIN has another competitive advantage, which is linked to its equity exposure in certain high-quality portfolio companies. When providing private credit, MAIN tends to co-invest equity to not only receive dividends but also (and mainly) to participate in the potential equity monetization event. This is a powerful instrument to diversify the returns and manage the downside that could stem from the struggling private credit area. For example, recently, MAIN monetized a stake with an annual internal rate of return of 72.2%, receiving 10 times the money invested return on its equity stake.

#2 - Kayne Anderson BDC

Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (NYSE:KBDC) is the largest BDC holding in my durable income-seeking portfolio. Currently, it produces a dividend yield of 10.7%, which is very high.

When seeing this kind of yield, the immediate reaction might be that the risk is definitely too high to qualify for a retirement income portfolio.

However, if we peel back the onion a bit, we will see why I am so bullish on the yield here (and the sustainability of it).

Here is why.

KBDC has a very well-structured portfolio. The average position size is below 1%, which neutralizes company-specific risks. The sectors are also nicely spread across different exposures, and, importantly, most of them are located in non-cyclical areas.

The portfolio itself is almost exclusively placed in the first-lien segment, where the investments have been backed by private equity partners (or sponsors).

While the aforementioned aspects are not that unique, they are still vital for long-term NAV and dividend sustainability. However, what is rather unique about KBDC is the following:

  • KBDC's portfolio company weighted average interest coverage is 3.1x, which puts the BDC in the sector's top decile, as for most of the peers the interest coverage stands between 1.6x and 2.2x.
  • KBDC has one of the lowest non-accruals in the sector—just 1.6% on a FV basis.
  • KBDC has an extremely conservative balance sheet at 0.85x, which will eventually become more leveraged, but that will also come with an incremental NII per share generation (i.e., boost the dividend coverage).
  • KBDC has no below-market level debt maturities to worry about, while for most of the closest peers, it is a significant issue.

In a nutshell, KBDC is a high-yielding BDC with the sector-leading fundamentals (especially, when it comes to pure-play first-lien BDCs). The dividend is, in my view, truly durable.

The bottom line

BDCs are inherently risk-on assets that do not directly fit into durable income-seeking portfolios. It is also, obviously, related to the retirement portfolios.

However, as in almost any asset class area, there are both good and bad exceptions.

In this case, I have identified two BDCs, which could be considered not only good exceptions but solid enough to qualify for a prudent income-oriented portfolio.

data.title: Insteel Industries: Growth Catalysts And Margin Tailwinds Appear Priced In
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Insteel Industries Inc. (NYSE:IIIN) is expected to experience revenue growth year-over-year in the coming quarters of FY2025, driven by higher volume and recent acquisitions of Engineered Wire Products and O’Brien Wire Products. Additionally, I believe a favourable price/cost spread should contribute to margin expansion in the future. Based on my estimates of free cash flow, cost of equity and constant growth rate, I believe that the company's fair value should be around $34. Although the prospects of the company look promising to me, I think they are already reflected in the current stock price.

Business Overview

Insteel is a manufacturer of steel wire reinforcing products for concrete construction applications. The products are prestressed concrete strand (PC strand) and welded wire reinforcement, including standard welded wire reinforcement (SWWR) and concrete pipe reinforcement (CPR). PC strand is a seven-wire strand that is used to create precast elements and structures. These products are used to reinforce bridges, parking decks, buildings and other concrete structures. WWR is either produced as standard reinforcing products or specially engineered products based on customer specifications. The company primarily operates in non-residential construction, which is 85% of its net sales, and the remaining 15% comes from residential construction.

Revenue Analysis & Outlook

In the second quarter of FY2025, the net sales of Insteel Industries stood at $160.7 million, an increase of 26.1% year-over-year. The growth was driven by increased shipments, partially offset by year-over-year average price decreases of 2.2%. However, the average price increased by 5.1% from the first quarter of FY2025. Moreover, the shipments were also increased from the previous quarter by 17.9%.

While most indicators of non-residential construction show a weak demand in future, the management of IIIN believes that the robust momentum from the previous quarter should continue in FY2025. The Dodge Momentum index was at 205.6 pts in March 2025, a decrease of 6.9% from the previous year due to volatility in material prices and tariff uncertainties. In commercial construction, the decline in activities for warehouses, data centres and retail stores caused the slowdown in the industry. Additionally, weak planning activities for education, healthcare and government buildings contributed to a decline in institutional construction. Moreover, the Architectural Billing Index (ABI) was at a 44.1 score in March, which is lower than its growth threshold of 50 points, indicating that the softness in the industry is likely to persist in the future. On the contrary, IIIN hold a strong order book and shipments increased by 28.9% year-over-year in the second quarter of FY2025. And Insteel’s quotation activities remain solid as its customers have a strong backlog. Along with this, an increased monthly spending by the U.S. Department of Commerce on construction should be a tailwind in the near term. The total construction spending for March was estimated at around $2196.1 billion, an improvement of 2.8% year-over-year. In addition to this, the monthly spend on Non-residential construction was estimated to be $1246 billion, up 2.9% from the previous year.

The correction of the Section 232 steel tariff anomaly should act as a growth catalyst for the company. Over the past few years, the company has been facing problems due to the 2018 Section 232 steel tariff, which was applied to most of the imported steel products, including the hot-rolled steel wires. However, this tariff strategy had a regulatory gap which does not impose the 25% tariff on the PC strand. The management has been trying to correct this anomaly for the past years, and now, recently, the new tariff regime includes the PC strand in its tariff strategy. Overall, 10% of Insteel’s sales are directly influenced by the offshore competitors, but with a recent correction in the tariff regime, I believe the company should use this timing to increase its market share in the construction industry.

Insteel’s latest acquisitions, Engineered Wire Products and O’Brien Wire Products, are expected to contribute to inorganic growth in the coming years. Both acquired companies manufacture Welded Wire Reinforcement (WWR) products used in residential and non-residential construction. The net sales of the former EWP facilities stood at $14.4 million for the quarter ending in March 2025; however, the exact net sales of EWP facilities are difficult to quantify as they have been integrated with Insteel's existing facilities.

Margin Analysis & Outlook

The gross margin of Insteel Industries stood at 15.3% in the first quarter of FY2025, an improvement of 300 basis points year-over-year, which was primarily driven by the volume leverage. Additionally, the reduction in conversion cost per ton by improved productivity also contributed to the margin. On the contrary, the Selling, General, & Administrative expenses negatively impacted the margins as it was 6.7% of net sales in the quarter, an increase of 50 basis points from the previous year.

For the outlook of Insteel's margins, it is important to understand the recent price/cost dynamics in the construction industry. The primary raw material of the company is the hot-rolled steel wire rod, which they source from either domestic or international producers. Recently, the management of Insteel has highlighted that two steel-producing mills have been permanently shut in the U.S., indicating supply chain constraints in the region. Moreover, Section 232 previously exempted Canada and Mexico from the tariff regime, but that exemption was revoked by the government. This should further tighten the supply of steel wire rod in the U.S. Given the supply chain constraints, I believe the cost of steel wire rod should increase in the future. During FY2024, the company has decreased its pricing due to the slowdown in demand for its products and competition from offshore steel producers. However, I have estimated that the demand should remain strong in the coming quarters. As demand strengthens, I think it is appropriate to believe that the company should be able to increase the price of its products to more than offset the higher input cost. This positive price/cost spread should be accretive to the gross margin in FY2025. In addition to this, the operating leverage and operational synergies from the recent acquisition of EWP and O’Brien Wire products should also contribute to improved profitability in the future.

Valuation & Conclusion

I have used discounted cash flow model for the valuation of Insteel Industries. As it is a cyclical company, I have projected financials over a 5-year forecast period. For the company, I assumed double-digit year-over-year revenue growth with an improving margin profile for FY2025, and then I have taken a long-term growth average of the industry as revenue estimates for the subsequent years. Free cash flow (FCF) in FY2025 is expected to be impacted by higher net receivables year-over-year; however, the benefits from the increased net accounts payable y-o-y should provide some support to the FCF. Moreover, I have used the cost of equity instead of weightage average cost of capital (OTC:WACC) as the company has no long-term debt and has an untapped revolving credit line. With a cost of equity of 8.7% and constant growth of 2%, I believe the fair value should be around $34. While it is true that I like the growth catalysts and improving margins of the company, I believe they are already reflected in the current stock price of $37. Therefore, I believe it is more appropriate for an investor to wait for a better entry position.

For calculating the cost of equity, I have used the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) with the following key inputs: a U.S bond 10-year yield of 4.54% as the risk-free rate and the market return of 10.82% based on the S&P 500 annualized return for the past 10 years. Moreover, I have used the Beta (monthly) of 0.66 in my estimates.

Risk

The company operates in a cyclical industry where the demand for its products is driven by the construction activity level, which correlates with the overall economy. In my forecasted results, I assumed there would be a strong demand for Insteel’s products in the coming quarters. However, if the macroeconomic conditions worsen, it may negatively impact the company and its financial performance. Moreover, I have projected a favourable price/cost spread for the coming year, which may be impacted by the availability of raw materials. A lower-than-expected availability of raw material could increase the input cost more than expected price increases. This scenario could negatively impact the gross margins in the future.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Low Profile
data.description:
The Federal Reserve continues to reduce the size of its securities portfolio but has reduced the number of securities it is removing every week.

In the last banking week, the banking week ending Wednesday, May 21, 2025, the size of the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright dropped by $6.5 billion.

Since September, the last time the Fed seemed to modify the speed at which it was removing securities, the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright dropped by just over $300.0 billion.

The Federal Reserve began its program of quantitative tightening in March 2022 and since March 16, 2022, the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright has fallen by $2,105.3 billion... or by just over $2.1 trillion.

Here is how this tightening appears graphically from March 16, 2022, to the latest banking week.

For 38 months, the Federal Reserve has kept up its program of quantitative tightening!

I am not familiar with any other program of economic policy that has lasted so long and so methodically. Mr. Powell and his troops have been very persistent in carrying out their current approach to monetary policy.

And, Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve set out to conduct this policy stance, with the Number 1 focus of the policy being the reduction of the size of the securities portfolio.

The number 2 focus was on the level of interest rates consistent with what the Fed was trying to achieve. Note: the level of the Fed's policy rate of interest was a residual goal...not the primary one.

So, we see that the Fed's policy rate of interest followed this particular pathway since March 16, 2022.

This period is defined by the Fed's effort of quantitative tightening.

But, there are three phases included within this period of quantitative tightening.

First, into the summer of 2023, when the Federal Reserve oversaw a rising policy rate of interest.

Second, from the summer of 2023 until the fall of 2024, a period when the effective Federal Funds rate remained constant and the economy was going through adjustment of sustained growth.

Third, a final period when the Fed was challenged to oversee a need to help reinforce an expanding period of economic expansion.

And, this is where we are now. But, how did the Federal Reserve achieve this changing interest rate scenario, given that the "thrust" of its monetary policy was still "quantitative tightening" but the economy was actually growing in excess of 2.0 percent?

Here is the commercial bank's position of holding "excess reserves."

One cannot draw a clear picture from this chart about what the Federal Reserve is trying to do over this period of time.

In the first period of time, from March 2022 into the second quarter of 2023, "excess reserves" fall.

The conclusion...the Federal Reserve is "tightening".

In the second period of time, from the second quarter of 2023 until the beginning of the second quarter of 2024, commercial banks have more "excess reserves" so one might think that the Federal Reserve is easing up on policy.

However, this was a time period that the effective Federal Funds rate was remaining constant.

In the third time period, since the second quarter of 2024, commercial bank "excess reserves" are dropping, is the Fed forcing the effective Federal Funds rate up?

No! The effective Federal Funds rate is falling, and the pressure from the market has been to lower this policy rate of interest further. But, it doesn't look, right now, like this is going to happen any time soon.

Conclusion....

The Federal Reserve is not committed to a further decline in the effective Federal Funds rate.

The Federal Reserve is continuing to reduce the size of the Fed's securities portfolio. That is the "Number 1" policy stance.

In terms of the effective Federal Funds rate...the Fed is trying to maintain a money market position that is consistent with its efforts to reduce the size of its securities portfolio.

The "stance" of the Fed is defined by what is happening to the securities portfolio.

We must analyze what is being done to the Fed's policy rate of interest in terms of how the Fed is managing the "excess reserves" of the commercial banking system in order to maintain the "long run" thrust of the Fed's monetary policy.

Right now, the "stance" of monetary policy is...keep reducing the size of the Fed's securities portfolio, but in a way that will not result in commercial banks falling into a liquidity dilemma.

The Federal Reserve has been able to carry on its quantitative tightening approach for 38 months without causing some kind of liquidity crisis. It obviously would like to continue this record until all the money pumped into the banking system during the Covid-19 financial crisis is removed.

The M2 money stock?

Here is how the M2 money stock has performed during this period of time.

When the Federal Reserve began its quantitative tightening, the M2 money stock initially declined.

But, in the summer and fall of 2023, the M2 money stock began to grow again. And, the Federal Reserve was still tightening...quantitatively.

Yet, as the economy grew, the money stock grew. Obviously, there were plenty of "excess reserves" in the commercial banking system to support the monetary growth...and continue to support economic growth.

And, this is why the Federal Reserve still needs to be concerned about all the "excess reserves" that exist within the commercial banking system.

The commercial banks have the money to expand...if the economic system demands it.

And, that is exactly what Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve face.

The commercial banking system is holding almost $3.3 trillion in CASH on its balance sheets!!!

The Federal Reserve system has almost $6.4 trillion in securities held outright on its balance sheet!!!

The banking system is not experiencing a liquidity problem.

The United States economy has LOTS and LOTS of money around to keep the economy...and the world...going!!!

However, it seems like the politicians want to see this CASH getting out into the public's hands, just as they want to see the government debt GROW and GROW and GROW!!!

data.title: Alphabet Is Wiping Out The AI Competition
data.description:
Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) has dominated tech for years, yet its recent run in artificial intelligence puts it on a different plane. Over the last 12 months Google has rolled out a one-two-three punch: its own AI models, chips it designed in-house, and a product lineup that already reaches billions. At Google I/O 2025, CEO Sundar Pichai showed how tightly those pieces snap together. The keynote mixed new model versions, fresh AI tricks inside everyday apps, and a peek at the custom silicon that makes the whole stack hum. The result is not just technological bragging rights but tangible business impact: Google’s core search business is growing robustly again and profitability remains high (operating margins hit 34% last quarter). The message to investors and the tech world is unmistakable: Alphabet’s all-in AI strategy is delivering, and it is doing so at a scale only Google can achieve.

Gemini: Google’s Home-Grown Model Hits a New Gear

Gemini has become the centerpiece of Google’s AI strategy. The in-house model family now drives everything from the core search engine to developer tools. At I/O 2025, CEO Sundar Pichai introduced Gemini 2.5, calling it the company’s smartest system so far. Gemini 2.5 Flash makes the case on its own. The streamlined version handles reasoning, coding, multiple languages, and mixed-media prompts more capably while operating at only a fraction of the cost of earlier releases. Google says smarter infrastructure and tighter tuning have driven AI expenses sharply lower, letting it sell top-tier models at the most competitive prices in the market.

A more powerful sibling, Gemini 2.5 Pro, is being enhanced with a “Deep Think” mode that uses parallel reasoning techniques to tackle especially complex queries, a capability Google touts as giving access to “the smartest model on the planet.” By one measure, Gemini’s AI performance has improved by more than 300 Elo points since the first generation, and the newest version is sweeping benchmark leaderboards. Crucially, these advances are not confined to text. Google’s AI model suite now spans images, code, and even video. At I/O 2025 Google rolled out Veo 3, its newest video generator that produces clips with native audio and spoken lines. In company trials users picked Veo 3 over competing models, including OpenAI’s test system Sora.

Imagen 4 brings the same step up to text-to-image work, pushing fidelity and detail higher while matching or beating OpenAI’s best model on speed. Google even developed a specialized model to power a new “Try-On” shopping feature that lets users virtually try clothes on their own photos. This is seemingly small, but proof of Google’s ability to spin up bespoke models for its product needs. From coding assistants (Google’s new code model “Jules” aims to outdo OpenAI’s Codex) to deep-research tools, the Gemini era is characterized by AI ubiquity across Google’s offerings. Usage is skyrocketing: Pichai noted that the Gemini app already boasts more than 400 million monthly active users, an enormous base that barely existed a year ago. Those users are not just dabbling; they are engaging deeply. Google’s systems now process fifty times more AI output tokens than they did at this time last year, a reflection of explosive growth in generative-AI usage. Scale at this level, with hundreds of millions of users and trillions of AI interactions, gives Google a powerful feedback loop to further improve its models and a built-in advantage that few can match.

TPUs: Custom Chips Fuel Google’s AI Edge

Gemini’s sprint forward owes a lot to Google’s home-grown Tensor Processing Units. While most labs still rely on off-the-shelf GPUs, Google has spent years making its own silicon. At I/O 2025 the company revealed Ironwood, its 7th-generation TPU built expressly for inference work. A fully loaded Ironwood pod pumps out 42.5 exaflops, topping the compute power of the fastest supercomputer on the planet. That punch translates into roughly 10x the speed of the prior TPU generation along with far better energy efficiency. The muscle behind Google’s TPUs, plus tweaks tailored for neural work, lets the company train and serve models faster and cheaper than anyone else.

Owning the silicon brings clear perks. First, it gives engineers room to chase larger, more capable networks; the deep-thinking routines inside Gemini 2.5 Pro rely on massive parallelism that only a dense TPU cluster can provide. Second, full-stack control means tighter tuning. Sundar Pichai says Google’s end-to-end infrastructure lets it roll out quicker models even as operating costs keep sliding, nudging the AI cost-performance curve in its favor. These chips also feed Google’s cloud business. TPU-powered AI services helped Google Cloud revenue jump 28% last quarter as more enterprises choose Google’s infrastructure. In short, TPU investment is not merely a technical win; it reinforces Google’s economic moat by making high-powered AI accessible at Google’s price and pace, something competitors struggle to match.

Unmatched Product Ecosystem: Scale, Integration, and Monetization

The third pillar of Alphabet’s AI dominance is its vast ecosystem of products and billions-strong user base. Google can take innovations in Gemini and deploy them instantly to audiences few, if any, rivals can reach. Nowhere was this clearer than at I/O 2025, where virtually every marquee product gained an AI boost. Consider Search: last year Google introduced AI Overviews, which are AI-generated summaries at the top of results, and the feature already reaches more than 1.5 billion users each month across two hundred countries. Rather than hurting core search, the new snapshot answers are lifting engagement. Wherever Overviews roll out, query counts climb.

Building on its recent gains, Google has launched AI Mode, a full conversational layer for Search. A new tab on the home page opens a chat-style panel where users can pose long, complex questions, refine them on the fly, and fold classic search links into the exchange. Early testers are submitting queries 2 to 3 times longer than usual and receiving answers packed with context. The feature begins rolling out to every U.S. account this week, showing how smoothly Google can weave cutting edge AI into tools people use every day. A standout example is Gemini Live, the culmination of Project Astra’s vision for a universal assistant that understands the world around you. Gemini Live allows users to share their phone camera or screen with the AI in real time and have a live conversation about it. Whether coaching a repair, translating street signs, or giving feedback on a tennis swing, the assistant “sees” what you see. The feature launched on Android immediately (iOS support is on the way), putting a powerful vision-based AI in billions of pockets.

The ecosystem advantage extends well beyond phones. AI features are seeping into Maps (Live View guidance), YouTube (AI-generated video summaries and search), and Workspace. Alphabet now counts 270 million paid subscriptions across YouTube Premium, Google One, and other services, with AI features set to enhance their value and spawn new subscription tiers. Google is also preparing “Agent Mode” in Gemini and Chrome, where AI can browse websites, execute multi-step tasks, and even book appointments. Such “AI butler” capabilities will open new monetization paths, from transaction commissions to premium tiers, while underscoring how integration across search, mobile, and web lets Google productize and monetize AI at massive scale. Financially, the payoff is already evident.

Financial Momentum

Alphabet’s latest quarter showed that its AI playbook is already lifting the numbers. Revenue reached $90.2 billion, up 12% year over year, while net income climbed 46% to $34.5 billion. Search revenue grew about 10% to $50.7 billion as AI Overviews drew 1.5 billion monthly users, proving generative features are enhancing rather than cannibalizing search. Google Cloud revenue jumped 28% to $12.3 billion and its operating margin nearly doubled to 17.8%, powered by demand for Gemini services on Google’s seventh-generation TPUs and the first Nvidia Blackwell clusters available in any public cloud.

Company-wide operating margin rose to 34%, the best in over a decade, thanks to AI-driven ad matching and tighter cost control. Robust cash generation remains a pillar: free cash flow over the past year was roughly 75 billion dollars, allowing Alphabet to authorize a 70 billion buyback even as it budgets about 75 billion for capital spending on AI and data centers this year. Despite these metrics, the stock still trades below the S&P 500’s average earnings multiple, and at a discount to Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META), leaving ample room for multiple expansion if Alphabet continues converting its AI leadership into faster growth and widening margins.

Risks and Challenges

Regulators are already circling. Google’s push to weave AI into every product is set to meet tough antitrust and privacy reviews worldwide. As these tools become part of daily routines, watchdogs will press the company on data stewardship and algorithmic fairness; any shortfall could bring rules that slow the rollout. Meanwhile, hyperscalers and AI research labs are throwing countless billions to build out their own comprehensive AI stacks. Competitors may not yet match Google’s all-in ecosystem, yet they are racing to close the gap. One very public AI slip-up could sap user confidence, and scaling ever-larger models carries a steep price tag. Alphabet must juggle rapid innovation with tight cost control if it wants to protect its margins.

Conclusion

A Bullish Trajectory Ahead Despite these challenges, Alphabet’s outlook in the AI era remains resoundingly bullish. Google I/O 2025 showed that the company has assembled an ecosystem nearly impossible to replicate: world-class AI models, vertical integration down to the silicon, and unparalleled reach to users and businesses. Google’s three-part advantage lets it move ideas from the lab into real products faster than any rival and collect the payoff. Sundar Pichai says that owning every layer of the stack is already lifting revenue and margins across Alphabet, and those tailwinds should strengthen as more AI-driven services reach the market.

With Android running on more than 3 billion devices and YouTube’s vast content library, Google can launch new features quickly, monetize them through ads, subscriptions, and cloud contracts, and set the standard for everyday AI utility. For investors and tech observers, Alphabet’s moves signal a company doubling down on innovation rather than resting on its laurels. If Google maintains the execution it displayed at I/O, it could define the next decade of AI much as it defined the last decade of search. As such, Alphabet is still relatively undervalued at a valuation of $2.08 trillion and forward P/E of 19, especially considering its growing lead in arguably the biggest technological shift in modern history.

data.title: IEF: Times Are Tougher, But Yields Aren't Denying It
data.description:
Bond market discussions are far and wide at the moment, especially given the fact that Moody's downgrade (last week) was succeeded by sluggish Bill and Bond auctions, which saw the 20-year fetch yields above 5% on Wednesday, May 21st.

After revealing our bullish outlook on longer-term bonds earlier this week, we decided to engage with the intermediate-to-long term of the curve by covering the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:IEF). We last covered IEF in March, stating it would be a good time to preposition into "flight to quality" assets amid an expectation that a risk-off zeitgeist will emerge in due course. Unfortunately, the vehicle has shed over 1.10% in total return since our analysis due to a myriad of reasons, possibly including U.S. fiscal concerns, a tariff debacle, ratings downgrades, and doubts about the sustainability of U.S. exceptionalism. While we acknowledge such concerns, we think IEF remains underestimated and that noise has dampened its interim performance, providing investors with an alluring opportunity to lock-in quality returns.

Without further ado, here's an extended discussion of our consensus.

Recent Performance

The iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF is up by about 2% in total return year-to-date. However, as illustrated in the diagram below, the ETF has suffered from significant price losses since reaching a peak in early April. Likely contributors include: Hard line tariff policies and how those policies will influence inflation, Moody's U.S. credit downgrade, and residual events such as market noise, implied global yield convergence, and anomalous factors.

A glance at IEF's optics shows that it isolates the intermediate term of the curve, investing in 7-to-10 year treasuries with a current effective duration of 6.96 years. The vehicle's effective duration may oscillate with time, but we think it's safe to claim that it won't drift far and wide away from 7-to-10 years. Moreover, IEF ETF has positive convexity and a minor (arguably negligible) option-adjusted spread, suggesting slight curvature and credit exposure risk. Given its market-based attributes, it makes sense why the vehicle has shed month-over-month value as 10-year yields spiked by circa 22.5 basis points.

Yields

Our view of U.S. treasuries might be quite controversial. We recently discussed the curve in an article about TLT ETF (TLT). That said, let's reiterate a few observations while shifting focus to the 7-to-10 year bucket.

As previously mentioned, U.S. 10-year yields have spiked. The term premium has drifted up to near 60 basis points, substantially higher than its sub-40 level in early April. In addition, U.S. 10-year Credit Default Swap spreads have skyrocketed to over 50 basis points from the 35 experienced in late February, adding to an interim credit risk premium (unusual for U.S. sovereign debt). Furthermore, real rates remain bulky at 167 basis points, substantially higher than the negative-to-low values experienced before that rate-hiking cycle started in 2022; the following diagram illustrates current U.S., 10-year yields, a discussion succeeds it.

So, the question now becomes: Where will U.S. Bond yields be when the dust settles? Let's emphasize the 10-year and 7-year to weigh in on the central question.

The U.S. has faced a series of downgrades since 2011, mostly due to fiscal matters, which we discussed in our article about TLT ETF. While fiscal concerns alongside higher-for-longer rates and inflation likely warrant higher risk premiums, the U.S. 10-year yield is the highest since late 2007. Thus, we could argue that risk premiums are compensating for the recent rise in risk factors. In other words: Yes, risks are likely higher, but yields have seemingly acknowledged that fact and now present investors with justified return opportunity.

We recently built up a yield curve based on where we think yields should be, deeming a 10-year yield value of between 4% and 4.10% fair. In addition, we think the 7-year should be materially lower than its current level. Our base case derives from the fact that weakening economic indicators (see the table at the end of this subsection) convey lower justified real rates, inflation has settled lower than most feared, the term premium will likely revert when market noise abates, and the CDS spike will probably decay when the Moody's downgrade and tariff debacles lose media attention. Considering the market-based sentiment, it might be unrealistic to assume that yields will drop to our justified levels, but we certainly expect a draw towards those levels in the coming quarters. Coinciding with that, we see economic data remaining sluggish for the remainder of 2025 (the U.S. has seemingly entered late cycle growth and global confidence is low), and the Fed walking down interest rates slower than they probably should, in turn, causing slight bullish flattening of the curve but not total inversion.

To conclude this section, I embedded a chart of economic data to accompany our curve discussion. Once more, if you're interested in an expanded discussion, kindly visit our Seeking Alpha page, which, on numerous occasions, reiterated a bearish economic outlook in recent months with our base case being that the U.S. is in late cycle growth and that tariffs will likely cause transmission into lower economic demand rather than inflation pass through.

Metric Current 1-Y Trailing
Quarterly U.S. GDP Growth -0.3% 1.6%
Leading Economic Index 99.4 Points 101.7 Points
Business Confidence 48.7 Points 48.8 Points
Consumer Confidence 50.8 Points 69.1 Points
Unemployment Rate 4.2% 3.9%
CapEx Index 27 Points 20.1 Points
10-Y CDS Circa 50.41 Circa 43.13
Inflation 2.3% 3.4%

Source: Trading Economics - Not All Metrics Are Measured In The Same Period, But All Are Y/Y Since Their Latest Recordings

IEF's Income, Expenses, and Quantitative Risk

The iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF's investors largely rely on distributions for total returns. The ETF isn't all that comparable to instruments such as ZROZ ETF (ZROZ), EDV ETF (EDV), or even TLT ETF, where high duration and convexity creates a speculative price environment. That said, IEF ETF has a maximum drawdown of 23.92% and recent history shows that its price returns are dominating proceedings for the time being. The ETF's income component does carry serious gravity, but we think a price adjustment is the real kicker for 2025.

Isolating distributions show that IEF ETF is a consistent payer with mild cyclicality. The ETF currently possesses a trailing yield of 4.43%. We expect the distribution yield to drop if interest rates fall. Yet, we expect IEF ETF's income component to deliver highly respectable interim results, given that interest rates are unlikely to settle far below 4% anytime soon (in our opinion).

The vehicle has an expense ratio of 0.15%, matching TLT's and exceeding GOVT ETF's (GOVT) by 10 basis points. We see no real issues in the ETF's expense ratio for the time being.

A Few Contra Points

Despite highlighting a few risks throughout the article, I thought it would be prudent to share our key concerns in a distinct section; herewith are a few risk factors worth considering.

  • We admit that yields can go higher, especially if fiscal dominance isn't directed toward the concerns of ratings agencies and institutions.
  • Our base case for tariffs leading to a demand slump can fall apart if tariffs are moderate (yet sustained) and confidence returns, which, would likely result in sustained or higher bond yields through an inflation spike.
  • As explained in our TLT ETF article, we think most fiscal concerns have already been priced by the bond market. However, we also believe the tax cut push by the GOP remains of concern, as it would likely dampen the U.S.'s ability to repay its interest obligations even further.
  • Though likely overblown by the media, U.S. exceptionalism is being challenged. Whether a realistic attempt or not, minor relative advantage depletion can send bond yields even higher.

Conclusion

Our core argument is: Sure, certain U.S. sovereign risks are higher, but they are being compensated for by higher bond yields. Moreover, we see real rates being higher-than-justified and inflation expectations ignoring a materialized demand-side backdrop among consumers and businesses. While acknowledging that risk premiums (yields) can go higher, we think yields will stabilize and eventually retreat when the noise settles down. In addition, sovereign debt finally reflects an investable risk premium, a factor that arguably wasn't present in recent years.

Considering our base case, we think IEF ETF presents a tangible opportunity to tap into a systematic risk premium, especially considering its idiosyncratic attributes, including a commendable income component, low-to-moderate risk, and a docile expense ratio.

Consensus: Very Bullish

data.title: Columbia Acorn Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Columbia Acorn Fund

The fund is positioned with a below-benchmark risk profile, while maintaining highergrowth and lower-leverage characteristics.

Fund strategy

An intrinsic value approach to growth investing

  • Employs a consistent and disciplined process to identify smalland mid-cap companies that possess economic value-creation potential not yet appreciated by the market
  • Seeks attractively valued companies that have strong management, positive and innovative cultures and operate within favorable market structures in growing sectors
  • Supported by an experienced analyst team, specialized by sector or industry, that follows an intensive bottom-up approach to stock selection

Expense ratio

Share class
No waiver
(gross)
With waiver
(NET)
Institutional 0.85% 0.82%
A 1.10% 1.07%
From the fund's most recent prospectus. Net expense ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver/expense reimbursement through 04/30/2027, unless sooner terminated at the sole discretion of the fund's board.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Acorn Fund returned −13.83% in the first quarter.
  • The fund's benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, returned −10.80% for the same period.

Overview and performance drivers

The first quarter of 2025 saw the Russell 2500 Growth Index decline - 10.80%, as a Trump 2.0 presidency proved to be shockingly different than Trump 1.0, with massive government efficiency and tariff announcements sparking fears of slower growth and persistent inflation amid shifting interest rate expectations. The quarter continued the trend of large-cap dominance over small caps, though the growth versus value performance gap narrowed somewhat. Sector performance showed significant dispersion, with energy, utilities and consumer staples holding up better, while information technology, consumer discretionary and industrials stocks faced meaningful headwinds.

The Columbia Acorn Fund underperformed the benchmark during the quarter. From a sector perspective, strong stock selection in financials and consumer staples contributed positively to relative return, while positioning in health care and information technology detracted from performance. Overall, stock selection rather than sector allocation was the primary driver of the fund's underperformance during the quarter.

Average annual total returns (%) for period ending March 31, 2025

Columbia Acorn Fund 3-m o. 1-year 3-year 5-year 10-year
Institutional Class -13.83 -10.02 -1.19 7.38 5.86
Class A w ithout sales charge -13.80 -10.23 -1.42 7.13 5.59
Class A with 5.75% maximum sales charge -18.74 -15.38 -3.33 5.87 4.97
Russell 2500 Grow th Index -10.80 -6.37 0.55 11.37 7.44
Performance data shown represents past performance and is not a guarantee of future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data shown. Please visit columbiathreadneedleus.com for performance data current to the most recent month end. Institutional Class shares are sold at net asset value and have limited eligibility. Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc. offers multiple share classes, not all necessarily available through all firms, and the share class ratings may vary. Contact us for details.

Columbia Acorn Fund

Top holdings (% of net assets) as of March 31, 2025
GCM Grosvenor (GCMG) 2.87
Churchill Downs (CHDN) 2.72
Ryan Specialty (RYAN) 2.47
SPX Technologies (SPXC) 2.36
BJ'S Wholesale Club (BJ) 2.24
Curtiss-Wright (CW) 2.19
TopBuild (BLD) 2.17
Colliers International Group (CIGI) 2.14
Parsons (PSN) 2.10
Paylocity (PCTY) 2.08
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.
Top five contributors - Effect on return (%) as of March 31, 2025
BJ'S Wholesale Club 0.49
Intra-Cellular Therapies 0.39
VSE Corp (VSEC) 0.32
Alignment Healthcare Inc (ALHC) 0.31
Ryan Specialty 0.29
Top five detractors - Effect on return (%) as of March 31, 2025
Globant (GLOB) -0.96
Glaukos (GKOS) -0.83
Parsons -0.76
Modine Manufacturing (MOD) -0.68
Five Below (FIVE) -0.63

Leading contributors

BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings is a membership-only warehouse club chain. We believe the company's membership model, under which customers pay to shop at its stores, provides greater durability to the business than is reflected in its valuation, making BJ's a quintessential classification misfit in our process, or a stock that is undervalued versus our estimate of future cash flows. Over half of the company's earnings are driven by membership fees, and, in return, its offerings provide significant value relative to traditional grocery stores. While the multiple has expanded, the valuation of the stock is closer to that of a grocery store than to Costco, its membership-only warehouse peer. The stock outperformed in the quarter due to a combination of strong financial performance, supported by growth in membership and store traffic, and a shift by investors toward more value-oriented and stable areas of the market.

VSE Corp has been one the fastest growing companies in the aerospace aftermarket industry in the last several years. It has continued to gain scale and develop deep relationships with some of the industry's largest manufacturers, such as Pratt Whitney, Honeywell and TransDigm. VSE reported a solid quarter, with positive results driven by record revenue growth in its core aviation segment. VSE also completed the acquisition of Kellstrom Aerospace and Turbine Controls, further expanding its product portfolio, technical capabilities and customer base, particularly in the high-margin engine segment. Further, the company announced an agreement to sell its lower-margin Fleet segment, marking a strategic shift toward becoming a pure play in the high-growth aviation aftermarket parts and services business.

GCM Grosvenor is a leading alternative asset manager that has underappreciated growth and stability supported by increases in assets under management and earnings that are largely driven by recurring fee-related revenue. The company has a strong management team and track record, and is benefiting from the secular growth of private market investing, including private equity and real assets. Grosvenor's valuation is below that of its peers, due in part to the market's failure to recognize the value of its Absolute Return Strategies (i.e., fund of hedge funds) business, which provides a point of differentiation vs. competitors. The stock outperformed in the quarter due to the company's strong financial performance, robust fundraising, growing carried interest earnings and visible and stable growth drivers, as well as an attractive valuation, in our opinion.

Leading detractors

Globant is a digital native IT services firm providing engineering, innovation and design services for its clients. The company is focused on enabling higher-value-add user experiences (rather than business processing) and services blue-chip customers like Disney, Formula 1 and the LA Clippers within its media and entertainment segment. The stock underperformed during the quarter due to disappointing results and a worse-than-expected 2025 growth outlook that included outsized weakness in Latin America. While macroeconomic uncertainty will likely linger over 2025 and could have a transitory impact on customers' IT budgets, we remain positive on the company's multi-year growth opportunity. Globant is well-positioned to capitalize, as artificial intelligence and digital transformation continue to drive accelerating technological change.

Parsons' underperformance in the quarter was largely due to concerns about reduced government spending, particularly under the Trump administration's Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) initiative aimed at cutting costs. DOGE has led to project cancellations for Parsons' federal and defense contractor peers, weighing on the stock in the quarter. We believe the market reaction was overstated, given the current environment of heightened geopolitical risks, including cyber terrorism. Parsons is a standout in the defense and infrastructure space, with a strong focus on cybersecurity, intelligence and critical infrastructure protection. With a 75-year history, the company has built an increasingly differentiated high-end talent base, addressing the systems and technology used to provide combat soldiers with an information advantage, complementing its legacy positions in missile defense and traditional federal services. We believe Parsons stands to benefit from efforts to increase efficiencies, while remaining well-positioned to benefit from tailwinds in cyber, AI, border control, infrastructure and environmental remediation.

Glaukos is the pioneer of the innovative field of micro-invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS). The company's iStent product line was first launched commercially in 2012 and is used to improve the drainage of eye fluid and reduce intraocular pressure, which is a key cause of glaucoma. The stock underperformed during the quarter, in part due to the broader sell-off of the past year's high-growth outperformers. In addition, Glaukos provided 2025 guidance that failed to live up to elevated investor expectations, partially due to an adverse Medicare coverage decision. We continue to believe in the company's substantial longterm opportunity and added to the position during the quarter. We believe Glaukos' newly approved drop-less platform (iDose) has the potential to double the company's revenue over the next few years and that the company's attractive financial profile with high gross margins and low capital requirements will lead to an inflection point in operating leverage and earnings.

Outlook

The first quarter of 2025 saw several market dislocations as investors adjust to the changing macro landscape. While a wide range of scenarios are possible, our investment philosophy and process remain consistent. While we are cognizant of the changing landscape, we are focused on making smart adjustments and not overreacting to daily headlines.

We remain growth investors focused on holding innovative and high-quality small-cap and mid-cap companies trading at discounts to their intrinsic value, or classification misfits. While our thesis can take time to play out in some instances, we believe market volatility provides an attractive opportunity to add to positions that have been unfairly penalized, or to add new holdings that previously didn't fit our investment criteria. We will continue to look to upgrade the portfolio in situations where the market's bark is likely worse than its bite.

While many of the companies we hold are idiosyncratic classification misfits, the fund is exposed to several key themes with long term secular tailwinds. These include:

Alternative asset managers: The portfolio maintains significant exposure to alternative investments, with holdings including GCM Grosvenor, Carlyle Group (CG) and Blue Owl Capital (OBDC). Demand for alternative investments continues to grow for both institutional and individual investors looking to diversify portfolios and potentially generate higher risk adjusted returns. Each of our holdings in this space addresses growing areas where they are leaders and have multiple other growth opportunities. Private equity and real estate are fairly well-established areas of private equity, with private credit, infrastructure and the secondary market continuing to gain traction.

Consumer stocks offering value or unique experiences: One of our top positions is Churchill Downs, a classification misfit that often trades in conjunction with the regional gaming companies but has a much broader business mix. At its heart is the irreplaceable Kentucky Derby asset, which it leverages across its various high-end businesses. At the other end of the spectrum, U.S. consumers have a tendency to treat value shopping as a sport, which we think positions BJ's Wholesale Club and Five Below to increase their share of consumer's wallets, particularly in times of inflation and economic weakness.

Defense: The trajectory of the U.S. defense spending and headwinds associated with the DOGE agenda have created some investor skepticism for the sector. Over the past several months, it has become increasingly clear that the current administration seeks to boost the Department of Defense budget to $1 trillion with a focus on modernization of inventory and systems and on nuclear capabilities. Further, Defense is largely insulated from tariff risks, given a largely domestic supply chain and cost-plus contract passthroughs. Within the portfolio, our holdings in the sector include Curtiss Wright, Parsons and AeroVironment (AVAV). Curtiss Wright is a best-of-breed compounder, with exposure to commercial nuclear as a potential growth catalyst. Parsons is a high-quality defense and infrastructure services contractor. AeroVironment is an innovative technology provider for unmanned aerial systems/drones.

In aggregate, the fund is positioned with a below-benchmark risk profile, while maintaining higher-growth and lower-leverage characteristics. The portfolio is a mix of companies currently thriving and those with the potential to rebound on better-than-expected results, as well as a combination of relatively defensive compounders and more cyclical, macro-sensitive holdings. We believe our exposure to a wide range of classification misfits has the fund well-positioned as we seek to achieve our goal of outperforming on a rolling three-year basis, while taking less risk.

Investment Risks

Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. Investments in small- and mid-cap companies involve risks and volatility greater than investments in larger, more established companies. Foreign investments subject the fund to risks, including political, economic, market, social and others within a particular country, as well as to currency instabilities and less stringent financial and accounting standards generally applicable to U.S. issuers. Risks are enhanced for emerging market issuers. The fund may invest significantly in issuers within a particular sector, which may be negatively affected by market, economic or other conditions, making the fund more vulnerable to unfavorable developments in the sector.

Commentaries available via email

Stay informed about your investments by subscribing to receive commentaries and other fund updates by email. Simply register with our subscription center and choose the publications you'd like to receive. We'll take care of the rest.

Subscribe

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. For a free prospectus or a summary prospectus, which contains this and other important information about the funds, visit columbiathreadneedleus.com. Read the prospectus carefully before investing.
