data.title: Hinge Health: I Have A Positive Hinge On This Offering
data.description:
Shares of Hinge Health (NYSE:HNGE) made a strong public debut. Not only did the shares price at the high end of the preliminary offering range, shares rose nearly 20% coming out of the gates, as investors like this use of technology in healthcare.

Strong growth, in fact accelerating growth, and profitable operations make me upbeat as well amidst a reasonable sales multiple at first sight. This makes me more excited to learn about the business going forward, with an intention to get involved if more becomes known.

A New Health System

Founded back in 2012, Hinge Health has a mission to build a new health system which looks to transform outcomes, experience and costs with the aid of technology to both scale and automate care.

The company aims to do this with software (of course including AI) to automate care for joint and muscle health, resulting in improved experiences, improved member outcomes and cost reductions. With software focusing on motion tracking, there is an electrical nerve stimulation wearable device involved as well, to create optimal exercises in an automated manner.

Applications include a wide spectrum of so-called MSK care, ranging from acute injury, to chronic pain and post-surgical rehabilitation.

MKS is a very prevalent group of conditions, with some 40% of the US population estimated to suffer from such an indication. Many of these require physical therapy, but many patients never get this because of difficulty to access, high costs and often end up having expensive and risky procedures performed instead.

The company furthermore believes that it is successful in introducing technology in health. The company recognized that it is not new in trying to do this, yet predecessors suffered from the fact that healthcare intervention includes many unstructured physical tasks, while the current healthcare reimbursement system has specific pathways.

By focusing specifically on physical therapy markets, the company can save over 90% on therapy hours, with innovative payment options provided, including direct sales to employers.

By the end of 2024, the company had over half a million members, from over 2,000 clients. The company generated some $390 million in revenues that year, which implies an average of just over $700 in revenues per member.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Hinge Health initially aimed to sell nearly 14 million shares in a $28-$32 range, with final pricing set at the higher end of the range. With some 8.5 million shares sold by the business, gross process from the offering are pegged at $272 million.

With a total of some 80 million shares outstanding (in various classes) the company is granted a $2.2 billion equity valuation at the offer price. Pro forma cash holdings come in near $400 million, although that much of this position is netted out, taking into account liabilities and preferred stock, as I work with an enterprise valuation assumption just north of $2 billion here.

The company generated revenues of nearly $293 million in 2023, on which operating losses of $130 million were reported (GAAP accounting). Revenues rose by a solid 33% to $391 million, with revenues up nearly a hundred million in dollar terms. The same degree of leverage was achieved on the bottom line, with operating loss narrowing to $32 million, making that real progress was made here.

Looking at the quarterly cadence, we see revenues (rounded at a midpoint) at $90 million, $101 million, $117 million and $124 million over the past fourth quarters, with the last quarter ending in March of this year. Moreover, the company posted operating profits in the two most recent quarter, posted at a sizable near $14 million number in the most recent quarter.

With revenues trending at half a billion and operating profits around $60 million, valuations are demanding, but the profitability and growth is very comforting. With a flattish balance sheet, a $60 million operating income number could yield after tax earnings around $50 million, for earnings around $0.60 per share. While an offering priced at 50 times earnings is demanding, it comes with impressive growth, with revenue growth accelerating a long way to the 50% mark in the most recent quarter.

For these reasons, shares rose to the $38 mark on the first day of trading. This pushed up the market value of the firm by roughly half a billion and based on a roughly $2.7 billion enterprise valuation, I see the business valued at around 5.5 times sales, and a 60 time earnings multiple.

These are high multiples, but the technology, business model and growth is impressive, which not automatically make me dismiss these valuation concerns here.

Concluding Thoughts

Risks for the company and this offering, like any offering or investment opportunity, are plentiful.

First and foremost is the valuation as quite some growth is priced into the stock, with this growth itself relying on high retention rates, reliance on relations with a few big PBMs, pricing for the services which it receives, and various classes of stock outstanding. One major source of concern is competition of course, as more players like Kaia Health, Omada Health, Sword Health and Vori health are active in similar markets.

Frankly, I am quite upbeat on the business, but not willing to chase at current levels, based on the current information. I look forward to the first quarterly results released as a publicly traded business to better understand the growth cadence and margin developments. For now, I have an upbeat undertone, but need to get more comfortable with the strength of the solutions and business model before committing capital to work.

data.title: XPeng Is Crushing It
data.description:
XPeng (XPEV) (OTCPK:XPNGF) reported better-than-expected results for its first-quarter due to growing momentum for the company’s deliveries. XPeng has trailed its rivals in the electric vehicle market in terms of both deliveries and vehicle margins in the last several years, but the EV maker is making great progress, especially with regard to deliveries. In the first-quarter, XPeng’s deliveries surged 331% year-over-year, due in part to a rapid upsurge of Mona-branded EVs, and the company saw a sequential improvement in its vehicle margins as well. While I like XPeng's progress so far this year, I am still cautious with respect to the EV company's valuation.

Previous rating

I rated shares of XPeng a hold in my last work (and before that) -- Growth And Margin Upside -- due to shares of the EV maker being highly valued relative to other electric vehicle companies in the market. Further, XPeng was lagging its EV rivals significantly in terms of vehicle margins. XPeng’s deliveries have surged lately, however, chiefly because of a positive market reaction for XPeng’s low-cost EV model Mona 03. I believe the current trajectory has improved greatly, but with XPeng still having inferior margins and a high valuation, I maintain my hold rating.

XPeng beats earnings

XPeng beat top and bottom-line predictions for its first fiscal quarter on Wednesday: the electric vehicle enterprise reported adjusted earnings of $(0.06) per-share, beating Wall Street’s consensus estimate by $0.15 per-share. Revenues came in at $2.2B, out-matching the average prediction by $17.4M.

Vehicle margin trend is improving

XPeng has lagged its rivals in terms of vehicle margins for a while and the company still has the lowest margins in the industry group. However, XPeng’s margins are improving and have surged in the last year. As of the end of the March quarter, XPeng posted a vehicle margin of 10.5%, showing growth of 0.5 PP quarter-over-quarter. In the last twelve months, XPeng’s margins have grown by a full 5.0 PP.

Li Auto (LI), a top contender in the Chinese electric vehicle start-up market, had a vehicle margin of 19.7% in the fourth-quarter of FY 2024 (results for Q1’25 have not yet been reported). NIO (NIO), another serious EV enterprise in China, had a fourth-quarter vehicle margin of 13.1% (results for Q1’25 are only due in the first week of June). While XPeng is still behind its rivals in terms of vehicle margins, the EV firm is making steady progress here which I believe works to the benefit of XPeng.

In the first-quarter, XPeng delivered 94,008 electric vehicles, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 331%. At no time in the EV maker's history did the company deliver more vehicles than in Q1’25. In April, the EV company delivered a massive 35,045 electric vehicles -- according to its monthly delivery report -- showing 273% year-over-year growth and XPeng delivered more than 30,000 units in each of the last six months.

This growth is partly fueled by last year’s release of the Mona M03 which has seen steady delivery growth since its launch. In April 2025, 14,210 vehicles were Mona M03 models, representing a massive 41% total delivery share.

XPeng is going to try to repeat this success when later this month it will launch the competitively-priced Mona M03 Max in China. The Mona M03 Max is a coupe sedan with an initial price sticker of 150,000 Chinese Yuan ($20,805) and is targeting specifically younger buyers below the age of 35. The Mona M03 Max will be equipped with XPeng’s Turing AI Driving Assistance System and allegedly has a range of 580 km on a single charge.

The Mona M03 is a low-cost EV model that appeals to buyers in China that are more price conscious, with an initial starting price of 119,800 Chinese Yuan (approximately $16,815). The Mona M03 was launched in late August 2024 and is meant to serve a market segment of younger and more budget-conscious buyers.

XPeng’s vehicle-related revenue surged 159% year-over-year to 14.37B Chinese Yuan ($1.98B) in Q1'25, mainly because of the success of the Mona M03 and a massively growing delivery share of this model. With sales and deliveries ramping up in the triple-digits in the first-quarter, XPeng also benefited from a margin boost: its gross margin reached an all-time high at 15.6%, showing a 1.2 PP quarter-over-quarter gain.

XPeng’s valuation

XPeng’s shares are still expensive, relative to its rivals in the Chinese EV start-up industry group. Shares of XPeng are currently valued at a forward (FY 2026) price-to-revenue ratio of 1.4X which makes the company about 43% more expensive than Li Auto and 158% more expensive than NIO. The industry average group P/S ratio is ~1.0X.

NIO has the lowest price-to-revenue ratio of 0.53X and is widely underpriced, in my opinion. In fact, EV companies that I like the most include BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) due to its massive export momentum and cheap valuation -- A Better Bet Than Tesla -- and NIO which is just massively undervalued given its opportunity for ONVO-related growth. ONVO is NIO's low-cost brand with which it tries to lure new, younger buyers to its EV business and I believe NIO has a decent chance of doubling its valuation going forward if it can maintain its current growth momentum. I also like Li Auto due to the fact that the EV maker is leading the industry group in terms of vehicle margins: Time To Be Greedy.

XPeng, on the other hand, is already quite expensive, relative to other EV companies, all of which have higher vehicle margins than the Mona-producer. Since Li Auto is leading the start-up group in terms of margins, I would choose Li Auto for profitability reasons. NIO is by far the cheapest EV play, so I see the biggest rebound potential with this EV maker.

Risks with XPeng

The biggest risk for XPeng is a potential slowdown in Mona-related deliveries which are currently boosting the company’s total delivery prospects. A weak market reception of the Mona M03 Max version later in May could also be a potential cause for concern for investors. The vehicle and gross margin trajectory, however, is looking encouraging. What would change my mind about XPeng is if the company were to see a massive slowdown in Mona deliveries and a weakening vehicle margin trend.

Final thoughts

XPeng reported better-than-expected first-quarter earnings on Wednesday that highlighted the considerable progress the company has made, especially in terms of ramping up its (Mona) deliveries and improving its gross and vehicle margin profile. While I like the EV maker's progress, XPeng’s shares are much more expensive than those of its direct EV rivals in the Chinese start-up group, which is why I remain cautious about the company. I like XPeng’s Mona M03 ramp and believe that the Max version could also be a success, potentially accelerating the company’s delivery and revenue growth. Shares have surged more than 153% in the last year and since they are now much more expensive than those of Li Auto or NIO, I maintain my hold rating here.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Not The Dip I Want To Buy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS), the stock has risen +35%, compared to around -2% for the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same period. That's quite an alpha, and not unjustified. Since then, the company has delivered good results and a good evolution in the thesis, ranging from the concrete delivery of good numbers to relevant partnerships with Novo Nordisk (NVO).

HIMS stock fell 5% on Wednesday and it was down another 7.7% on Thursday due to Cigna's (CI) deal with Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly (LLY). Unlike the Q4 dip mentioned in my first article, this new dip is a move where I don't think it's a great opportunity.

This Deal Isn’t Good News For Hims & Hers

A few weeks ago, Hims & Hers announced its collaboration with Novo Nordisk. In the company's own words, this is a “long-term collaboration”, and aims to offer more affordable obesity care and treatments. This is a huge step forward for the thesis, which previously seemed to be a threat to the company and show that the platform was not a moat, now shows that even very consolidated pharmaceuticals want to participate together with Hims & Hers.

Now, the narrative has changed somewhat. Cigna will now offer weight-loss drugs like Wegovy and Zepbound for $200 a month, which is cheaper than Hims & Hers' offer, and much cheaper than Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly's price.

On the positive side, it's still possible to argue that GLP-1 drugs don't sum up Hims & Hers' thesis. The company is much more than that, and continues to execute its strategy well, adding more products, with strong growth in subscribers, revenue (as the chart below shows) and this converting into cash flow.

But it's undeniable that this Cigna deal reveals some of the risks of the thesis. It is a direct competitive risk, since Hims offers branded and compounded drugs. There is a decrease in the use of alternatives offered by Hims & Hers with the cost of the drugs at $200/m through Cigna, i.e. it eliminates one of the differentials that was price, practicality and the like. There is also a derivative of this risk, since with more competition, the company may gain fewer subscribers who would come because of this solution.

But in my opinion, more than the actual risk, this shows that the scenario is not static, quite the opposite. This market is constantly evolving and is very dynamic, with different partnerships and players emerging all the time, as well as changes in regulation and players adapting. This needs to be mentioned and needs to be considered in the analysis, there is a relatively large level of uncertainty, even though Hims & Hers is still doing a good job.

The Positive Points of the Thesis Continue

The positive points of the thesis continue. The company still has strong potential in its hands: to increase what it offers on the platform, to continue with good services, to bring in more customers and to increase revenue per customer. It's similar to theses like that of Nu Holdings (NU), whose core is to grow ARPAC, grow customers, but keep the cost to serve low. In the case of Hims & Hers, scale will also be very important and should continue to help generate cash.

The table below summarizes the company's strategy well. First it needs to acquire new customers, and it does this via ambassadors, partnerships, advertising (as seen at the Super Bowl); then it needs to retain these customers, and this is done through user experience and the like. If the customer has come to Hims for an obesity treatment, for example, if they see that it really is a company that offers a good, practical and cheap service, they may also opt for hair or various other treatments (which will still be expanded) that Hims & Hers offers. Finally, engagement increases revenue per subscriber.

Even with the ups and downs of GLP-1, Hims & Hers has not only created a great platform, but above all has the potential to continue growing and consolidating.

But HIMS' Valuation Has Worsened

Okay, the positive points of the thesis remain, but we must remember the uncertainty that comes with this market and this business model highlighted in the first section. The third relevant point, and the one that will be the “icing on the cake” to justify my downgrade, is valuation.

In Q4, annualized revenue per subscriber was $671. In Q1, this metric went up to $970, with the company's new offerings. Now, it doesn't seem so far-fetched to estimate that by 2027 this revenue per subscriber could be just over $1,000 a year. If it advances by around 4% a year (and maintains this strong base), it will be enough to reach something close to $1,100. This seems a reasonable assumption to me, since it takes into account a strong base and the ability to upsell and cross-sell, but it's also not overly optimistic due to the dynamism of the market.

As for the number of subscribers, I rounded the assumption up to 4 million. To achieve this by 2027, Hims & Hers will have to add around 600,000 customers a year, which also seems reasonable, and even conservative, given that they are currently adding something close to 200,000 per quarter.

Finally, I kept the 13.5% net margin assumption. With these adjustments, we now have an estimated net income of $594 million in 2027. This net income implies a price-to-earnings ratio of 20x.

Well, for a company with a lot of potential, 20x is not expensive. But note that one of the main changes in this estimate was the market cap, which even after these falls is $12 billion.

There is still the potential for Hims & Hers to continue to surprise, perhaps 4 million subscribers is just the beginning. Its total addressable market is gigantic, so it's not crazy to imagine that in a few decades this number could be several million. If that happens, the revenue will certainly be higher, the net margin will be higher, and consequently, the shareholder value generated will be much higher. In other words, in a still bullish scenario, Hims & Hers is still fairly priced.

However, there's no denying that the increase in the price of the stock in recent months, together with the dynamics of the market, reduced the margin of safety.

The Bottom Line

In view of the above information, although I still believe in the potential of Hims & Hers, I prefer to opt for a more neutral position at the moment. The current situation of greater uncertainty and an already relatively high market cap, reduces the margin of safety and consequently causes me to downgrade the rating.

Note that the “hold” here is not in an overly pessimistic tone, but because I believe that this environment is not enough for an optimistic “buy” rating, I opted for the downgrade.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
